items.title: Software reasoning engine
items.description: A software reasoning engine is the component that decides what to build and how, by combining curated project memory, a structured view of the existing code, and deterministic validation, rather than relying on free-form generation alone. It treats a language model as one advisor among several inputs and always grounds decisions in retrieved facts.
items.applicability: Load this definition first whenever LocalAIFactory plans, debugs, or extends software so that reasoning is grounded in memory and code structure, not improvisation.
items.example: Asked to add a payment endpoint, the engine retrieves approved knowledge on posting rules, inspects the code graph for an existing GL posting service, and only then drafts a plan that reuses that service.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: the engine must orchestrate retrieval, code-graph lookup, and validation as explicit steps, not bury them inside one model call. Test implication: assert that a plan cites at least one retrieved fact and one code-graph node before proposing changes. Generator implication: generators consume the engine's grounded plan, never raw model text. Honest boundary: the engine improves grounding but cannot guarantee correctness; deterministic validation remains the final authority.

items.title: Code graph
items.description: A code graph is a queryable structure of code facts: nodes for projects, files, types, methods, and entities, with edges for calls, references, inheritance, and ownership. It lets the platform answer structural questions (who calls this, what depends on that) without re-reading entire files.
items.applicability: Used whenever the platform needs to locate where a capability already exists or to assess the blast radius of a change before editing.
items.example: Before adding logging to authentication, a query for nodes of type method with an edge into the login service returns the exact call sites to touch.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: extract the graph from source on ingestion and store it so queries are fast and offline. Test implication: verify that a known call relationship appears as an edge and that deleting a file removes its nodes. Generator implication: generators query the graph to find reuse targets instead of regenerating existing code. Honest boundary: a static graph can miss reflection, dynamic dispatch, and runtime-only relationships.

items.title: Graph retrieval
items.description: Graph retrieval is the act of pulling a focused subset of the code graph relevant to a task and injecting it into the reasoning context. It selects the neighbourhood around the target nodes (callers, callees, related entities) rather than dumping the whole graph.
items.applicability: Applied on every build or debug task to give the reasoning engine just enough structural context to act safely within a bounded prompt.
items.example: For a request to modify invoice posting, graph retrieval returns the invoice entity, the posting service it calls, and the two reports that read its output, and nothing else.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: bound the retrieved neighbourhood by depth and size so context stays within model limits. Test implication: assert that retrieval for a node includes its direct dependents and excludes unrelated subsystems. Generator implication: the generator plans only against retrieved nodes, preventing edits to unseen code. Honest boundary: too-narrow retrieval can hide a relevant dependency, so retrieval depth is a tunable trade-off, not a solved problem.

items.title: Safe model-assisted reasoning
items.description: Safe model-assisted reasoning is the practice of using a language model to suggest, summarise, or draft, while treating every output as a proposal that must be checked against retrieved knowledge and deterministic validation before it is trusted. The model accelerates work; it does not certify it.
items.applicability: The default mode for any task that involves a model, ensuring its fluency never substitutes for verification.
items.example: A model proposes a regex for input validation; the platform runs it against a test corpus and a security check before any code keeps it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: wrap model calls so outputs are labelled proposals and routed to validation, never written directly to disk. Test implication: assert that an unvalidated model suggestion cannot reach a committed file. Generator implication: generators accept model text only after it passes the same gates as human-written code. Honest boundary: validation reduces but does not eliminate the risk of plausible-but-wrong model output.

items.title: Code building block
items.description: A code building block is a named, reusable unit of solution knowledge that captures a recurring problem and its proven shape: the inputs it needs, the files it produces, the security and validation rules it enforces, and the tests that prove it. It is a brick the platform composes, not a copy-paste snippet.
items.applicability: Retrieved whenever a task matches a known problem so the platform reuses a vetted block instead of regenerating a solution from scratch.
items.example: A login request matches the secure-login block, which specifies the controller action, the hashing rule, the lockout rule, and the Playwright proof to emit together.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store a block as structured metadata plus a pattern summary, not as frozen literal code, so it adapts to context. Test implication: verify a block declares at least one test pattern and one failure mode. Generator implication: the generator instantiates a block by filling its required inputs. Honest boundary: a block encodes a pattern, not a guarantee of fit; mismatched context still requires human judgement.

items.title: Generator template
items.description: A generator template is the parameterised recipe that turns a code building block plus its required inputs into concrete files. It defines the placeholders, the output paths, and the post-generation validation to run, so output is consistent and verifiable rather than ad hoc.
items.applicability: Used at the emit stage of any build task, after the plan and the matched blocks are chosen.
items.example: The CRUD template takes an entity name and field list and emits a controller, two views, and a service test in fixed locations, then triggers a build check.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep templates deterministic so the same inputs always produce the same files. Test implication: snapshot a template's output for fixed inputs and assert it does not drift. Generator implication: the generator selects a template by block, never invents file layout. Honest boundary: templates capture structure well but cannot encode every domain nuance, so generated output still needs validation and review.

items.title: Experience memory
items.description: Experience memory is the durable record of past attempts: what was tried, what passed, what failed, and why. It lets the platform avoid repeating known mistakes and prefer approaches with a track record, turning each task into evidence for the next.
items.applicability: Consulted before planning so the engine can favour previously successful approaches and warn about previously failed ones.
items.example: A new import task retrieves an experience entry showing a prior import failed without a rejected-row report, so the plan includes that report from the start.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: record each attempt with its outcome and a link to the evidence, not just a success flag. Test implication: assert a failed attempt is retrievable by the same problem signature later. Generator implication: generators weight templates by their experience-backed success rate. Honest boundary: experience memory reflects only what has been observed locally and can encode survivorship bias if failures are under-recorded.

items.title: Safe agent runner
items.description: A safe agent runner is the controlled executor that carries out planned steps inside policy limits: bounded tool access, no destructive actions without approval, and full logging. It separates deciding from doing, so an agent's actions are constrained and auditable.
items.applicability: Wraps every automated action the platform takes, especially any that touch the filesystem, database, or shell.
items.example: An agent step that would drop a column is intercepted by the runner, flagged as destructive, and held for human approval instead of executing.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the policy in the runner itself, not in prompt instructions the model could ignore. Test implication: assert a destructive command is blocked and logged without executing. Generator implication: generated automation runs only through the runner. Honest boundary: a runner constrains actions but cannot judge intent, so policy must be conservative and default to refusal.

items.title: Model router
items.description: A model router is the component that selects which model (or no model at all) handles a given task, based on the task profile, availability, and cost. It enables fallback-first behaviour: deterministic paths first, then a local model, never a hard dependency on any single engine.
items.applicability: Invoked for every task that might use inference, deciding whether to use a model and which one.
items.example: With Ollama absent, the router selects the deterministic template path and the work still completes, logging that no model was used.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: the router must have a no-model fallback for every routed capability. Test implication: assert each task profile completes with all models disabled. Generator implication: generators request a capability, not a specific model, so routing stays swappable. Honest boundary: routing decisions are heuristic and a poor profile match can pick a weaker path; routing does not improve any individual model's quality.

items.title: Deterministic validation
items.description: Deterministic validation is the set of repeatable, non-model checks that decide whether work is acceptable: a build gate, a unit/integration test gate, a security gate, and a Playwright end-to-end gate. The same inputs always yield the same pass or fail verdict, making validation the authority over any model opinion.
items.applicability: Run after any change the platform proposes and before anything is accepted, proven, or proposed for approval.
items.example: A generated feature must compile, pass its xUnit tests, clear the security scan, and pass a Playwright flow; failing any gate blocks acceptance.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: implement each gate as an independent, scriptable step with a clear pass/fail exit. Test implication: assert that failing one gate fails the whole verdict. Generator implication: generators are only trusted after all gates pass. Honest boundary: gates verify only what they cover; absence of a test for a behaviour means that behaviour is unproven, not proven correct.

items.title: Production readiness ladder
items.description: The production readiness ladder is the ordered set of rungs a piece of work climbs before it is considered shippable: it builds, it is tested, it is secure, it passes end-to-end proof, it survives a fresh clone, and it passes a smoke test. Readiness is the highest rung reached, not a self-declared status.
items.applicability: Used to report honestly how far a feature or product has progressed toward production.
items.example: A module that builds and passes unit tests but has no Playwright proof sits on the test rung, and is reported as such rather than as production-ready.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: track and display the current rung explicitly per artefact. Test implication: assert a rung cannot be marked reached unless its gate actually passed. Generator implication: generators report the rung achieved, never inflate it. Honest boundary: the ladder measures verification coverage, not real-world operational fitness such as load or data scale.

items.title: ERP document lifecycle
items.description: The ERP document lifecycle is the controlled state machine a business document moves through: Draft, Submit, Approve or Reject, and after posting only Cancel or Reverse. Once a document is posted it becomes immutable; corrections are made by a reversing entry, never by editing history.
items.applicability: Governs any generated ERP document (invoice, payment, journal, stock movement) so financial records stay auditable.
items.example: An approved invoice cannot be edited; to correct it the system posts a reversal and a fresh corrected invoice, preserving the original trail.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce allowed transitions in a single domain method and forbid mutation of posted rows. Test implication: assert editing a posted document is rejected and a reversal produces offsetting entries. Generator implication: lifecycle-bearing entities are generated with the state machine, not free-text status. Honest boundary: the lifecycle enforces structure, not the business correctness of any individual posting.

items.title: Authentication hardening
items.description: Authentication hardening is the baseline set of defences applied to any sign-in path: salted, iterated password hashing (such as PBKDF2), account lockout after repeated failures, an enforced password policy, anti-forgery tokens on state-changing forms, and an audit trail of authentication events. It is the floor, not an optional extra.
items.applicability: Applied to every login feature the platform generates or reviews.
items.example: A generated login stores PBKDF2 hashes, locks an account after five failures for a cool-down window, and writes an audit event for each attempt.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never store plaintext or fast-hashed passwords and never disable anti-forgery on POST forms. Test implication: assert lockout triggers, weak passwords are rejected, and audit rows are written. Generator implication: the login block always emits these defences together. Honest boundary: hardening raises the cost of attack but does not replace transport security, secret management, or threat-specific review.

items.title: Migration safety
items.description: Migration safety is the discipline of evolving a database schema predictably: a design-time DbContext factory so tooling can build the context, applying migrations with Migrate on a real SQL Server while using EnsureCreated only for an ephemeral SQLite test database, and applying schema from the deterministically emitted migration rather than ad hoc scripts. Changes default to additive and backward-compatible.
items.applicability: Governs every schema change the platform proposes or generates.
items.example: A new column is added through an additive EF migration applied with Migrate at startup; a SQLite test run uses EnsureCreated against the same model and never touches production.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: do not mix EnsureCreated and Migrate against the same real database, and keep the model snapshot consistent. Test implication: assert migrations apply cleanly on an empty database and are idempotent. Generator implication: generated schema changes ship as migrations, not raw SQL. Honest boundary: additive safety covers common cases; genuinely destructive changes still require explicit human approval and a backup.

items.title: Playwright proof
items.description: A Playwright proof is an end-to-end browser test that drives the real running application through a user flow and asserts the observable outcome. It is evidence that a feature works as a user would use it, not just that code compiles or units pass.
items.applicability: Required to claim a user-facing feature actually works, and to climb the end-to-end rung of the readiness ladder.
items.example: A login proof opens the page, submits valid credentials, and asserts the dashboard loads, then submits bad credentials and asserts the error and lockout behaviour.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: run the proof against a freshly started instance with a known seed so results are repeatable. Test implication: assert the proof fails when the flow is broken, not only when it passes. Generator implication: user-facing blocks emit a Playwright pattern alongside the code. Honest boundary: a proof covers the scripted path only; unscripted flows and edge cases remain unverified.

items.title: Fresh-clone proof
items.description: A fresh-clone proof is a verification that the project builds, migrates, and runs from a clean checkout with no hidden local state. It catches reliance on uncommitted files, machine-specific configuration, or undocumented setup that would break for a new user.
items.applicability: Used before claiming a deliverable is portable or ready for handover.
items.example: Cloning the repo into a new folder, restoring, building, and starting the app succeeds without any manual fix-up, proving setup is fully captured.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: run setup from the committed scripts only, with no manual steps. Test implication: assert the clean-clone build and startup succeed in CI or a scripted drill. Generator implication: generated setup must be self-contained in committed files. Honest boundary: a fresh-clone proof validates portability of setup, not behaviour under production load or real data volumes.

items.title: Fallback-first design and model-is-not-the-authority design
items.description: Fallback-first design means every capability has a working path when optional services (model, vector store, internet) are absent, so the core always functions. Model-is-not-the-authority design means a model may propose and review but never decides acceptance; deterministic validation and human approval hold final authority. The two together keep the platform reliable and trustworthy.
items.applicability: A cross-cutting principle that shapes how every feature, dependency, and automated decision is built.
items.example: With no model and no vector store, the platform still loads its pages, retrieves approved memory from SQL, and generates from templates; a model's later suggestion is gated before it counts.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate every optional service behind a flag with a graceful default, and route model output through validation. Test implication: assert core flows pass with all optional services disabled and that a model verdict alone cannot accept work. Generator implication: generators target the fallback path first and treat model enhancement as additive. Honest boundary: fallbacks may offer reduced capability, and removing the model removes assistance, not the requirement to verify.
